name: Lamashtu
description: Lamashtu was a female demon, monster, goddess (depending on interpretation) in Mesopotamian mythology, particularly feared in ancient Assyria and Babylonia. She was believed to prey upon pregnant women, infants, and young children. Described as having a lion's head with donkey's teeth and ears, long fingers and fingernails, and often depicted standing or kneeling on a donkey, she was a terrifying figure associated with disease, miscarriage, and sudden infant death. Lamashtu was believed to fly through the air, attacking those who were vulnerable. Countermeasures against her included amulets depicting Pazuzu, her demonic antagonist, and incantations recited to ward off her evil influence. Despite her malevolent nature, some scholars argue that Lamashtu was also viewed as a goddess with the power to both inflict and protect, highlighting the complex and often contradictory nature of Mesopotamian deities. She was considered the daughter of the sky god Anu, but this divine lineage did little to temper her destructive tendencies. Her influence was pervasive, and numerous texts detail rituals and prayers designed to appease or repel her, underscoring the significant role she played in the anxieties and beliefs of ancient Mesopotamians. Lamashtu's power extended beyond physical harm, encompassing psychological terror and the disruption of familial well-being, making her a particularly dreaded figure in Mesopotamian households. The emphasis on protecting pregnant women and infants against her attacks reflects the high rates of mortality during childbirth and infancy in that era.
subtitle: Demoness of childbirth and infancy.

keyMyths.title: Lamashtu's Attacks
keyMyths.description: Lamashtu was believed to target pregnant women and newborn infants, causing miscarriage, stillbirth, and infant death. People used amulets and incantations to protect against her attacks.

keyMyths.title: Pazuzu's Protection
keyMyths.description: Pazuzu, a wind demon, was often invoked as a protector against Lamashtu. Amulets depicting Pazuzu were placed near women and children to ward off her evil influence.

keyMyths.title: Lamashtu's Depiction
keyMyths.description: Lamashtu's monstrous appearance, with a lion's head and donkey's features, was meant to inspire fear and highlight her destructive nature. Her depiction reinforced the need for protection against her.

sources.author: Thorkild Jacobsen
sources.work: The Treasures of Darkness: A History of Mesopotamian Religion
